title: Source Editor Add Image And Add File
summary: Publish staged image or download media and insert its logical reference into the document being edited, with shared SVG publication for HTML import.
content_html:
Source Editor Add Image And Add File

Outcome

An author editing Markdown can publish staged media to the current scope and insert the correct logical reference without creating a wrapper document or choosing a storage provider.

The source editor exposes two distinct actions:

  • Add image… publishes a supported raster image or sanitized SVG, or turns staged Mermaid source into canonical .mmd plus verified SVG, then inserts Markdown image syntax with useful alt text.
  • Add file… publishes an allowlisted opaque download and inserts a labelled Markdown link.

Docs Import remains for material interpreted as document content: HTML, Markdown, text, trusted document packages, and their embedded media. Standalone image, SVG, and download files belong to these source-editor actions rather than Docs Import.

Authoring Flow

Each action starts from the active source editor and scope:

  1. list only compatible direct-child files from the shared staging root;
  2. choose one staged file and collect image alt text or a download label;
  3. internally resolve its logical media identity and existing-media collision;
  4. publish and byte-verify through the scope's registered media location;
  5. after success, replace the current selection or insert at the caret;
  6. leave the source buffer dirty so Rebuild doc remains the canonical source-write boundary.

Publication precedes token insertion. Abandoning the later source edit may leave an unreferenced media object, but cannot commit a reference to bytes that failed to publish. Staged-source cleanup remains a separate explicit action.

New or byte-identical media proceeds without a second confirmation. Changed bytes using an existing identity prompt Replace or Cancel and show that stable logical identity, not repository, external-local, or R2 details. SVG sanitizer removals can also prompt review before publication. Replacing media does not replace a document or allocate a document identity.

SVG And Embedded-Media Contract

SVG is a distinct published media class reached through the same Add image… author action as raster img. One shared sanitizer handles staged SVG and inline <svg> found while Docs Import converts HTML. It preserves safe self-contained styling and fragment references, strips scripts, event handlers, processing instructions, and external references, and returns sanitized bytes plus title/alt assistance and diagnostics.

  • A staged standalone SVG uses a stable token-safe media identity derived from its staged basename; already-safe basenames remain unchanged.
  • SVG extracted from imported HTML uses deterministic <canonical-doc-id>-image-NN.svg identities in document order.
  • HTML import sanitizes the original SVG fragment so case-sensitive element names, namespaces, and stylesheet text are not corrupted by HTML-to-Markdown serialization.
  • HTML import publishes and verifies extracted SVG before writing Markdown image tokens.
  • Manually authored inline SVG remains an expert exception; existing inline SVG and wrapper documents are not migrated automatically.

The same provider-neutral publication boundary serves raster images under img, sanitized vector images under svg, and opaque downloads under files. Each class has its own configured logical prefix, physical location, and served path; the editor action does not expose that distinction as another author choice. Historical SVG already published beneath img remains valid and is not migrated or aliased.

Wrapper-Import Retirement

Standalone raster image, SVG, and downloadable-file sources have been removed from Docs Import together with their wrapper-document preview and apply paths. Docs Import continues to extract or publish media that belongs to interpreted document content, including HTML, Markdown packages, and trusted collections.

An author who wants a media-only document can explicitly create a document and then use Add image… or Add file…. That composition does not justify a second implicit wrapper-document workflow.

Mermaid Build-Source Contract

Mermaid extends Add image… without changing its editor ownership. The service lists staged .mmd, renders through the registered repository-pinned producer before configured writes, retains the staged file, copies canonical source into the scope's build-media root, publishes and verifies same-basename SVG, and returns only the SVG token for insertion into the dirty buffer. Ongoing edits use the diagram-title link in rendered Document Info's Diagrams section and Docs Watcher rather than staging the source again.

Document Diagrams owns the source/build/reader contract and repository workflow.

Boundaries

  • No asset library, upload history, automatic orphan deletion, version history, or rollback copies.
  • No repository/R2 choice in the editor actions; scope configuration owns physical placement.
  • Existing published media can still be referenced by typing or pasting its logical token.
  • Semantic references remain separate because they create registry-backed Work, Series, or Moment relationships.
  • Mermaid rendering remains owned by Document Diagrams and publishes sanitized SVG through the same image-media boundary.

Delivery

Source Editor Add Image And Add File Delivery owns implementation and verification.

Durable Home

Docs Images And Assets owns the author workflow, Media And Asset Handling owns publication and sanitization, Source Editor Endpoints owns the editor boundary, and Docs Import Architecture owns embedded-media extraction and wrapper-import retirement.
